description: Architectural rules enforcing modular, single-responsibility React components. Prevents monolithic 'God Components' that are impossible to maintain or test.
enforcedBy: {{PROJECT_NAME}} AI Staff Engineer — enforced during code generation, review, and refactoring.
philosophy: A component should do exactly one thing and do it well. If it does too much, it must be decomposed before merging.

_ai_agent_instructions.CRITICAL: Before generating any React component, check: (1) Does it exceed 150 lines? (2) Does it have more than one responsibility? (3) Does it have more than 3 useEffect hooks? If any answer is YES, decompose it first.
self_check_before_output: Count lines of the component you just generated.
List the responsibilities of the component — there must be exactly ONE.
If any limit is exceeded, extract to sub-components or custom hooks before outputting.
_ai_agent_instructions.decomposition_trigger: If you catch yourself writing 'and also' when describing what a component does, it has more than one responsibility. Split it.

rules.name: Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)
rules.description: Every component must have a single, well-defined responsibility. It should handle EITHER UI presentation, state logic, OR data fetching — never all at once.
guidelines: If a component fetches data AND renders a complex list, split into a container (data) and presentational (UI) component.
A component's name must clearly describe its single purpose. 'UserProfileCard' is good. 'UserStuff' is a violation.
If you find yourself adding 'and also' when describing what the component does, it has too many responsibilities.

compliant: UserProfileCard — renders a user card UI from props only
useUserData — fetches and manages user data state
PostComposerForm — renders and validates the post creation form
violations: UserDashboard — fetches users, updates state, renders list, AND handles pagination in one 400-line file
MainPage — contains business logic, API calls, form handling, and rendering in one component

rules.consequence: Violation triggers mandatory decomposition before the PR can be merged.

rules.name: Component Size Hard Limits
rules.description: Components must not exceed defined size limits. These are enforced as build gates, not guidelines.

softLimit.action: Triggers refactoring review. The AI agent must propose a decomposition plan.

hardLimit.action: BUILD FAILURE. The component must be refactored before any output is accepted.

maxUseEffectHooks.action: More than 3 useEffect hooks is a strong indicator of too many responsibilities. Extract into a custom hook immediately.

examples: Rendering a form section — ONE responsibility
Managing a piece of state — ONE responsibility

rules._ai_agent_note: Count your lines BEFORE outputting. If you are at 200 lines, STOP and decompose. Do not output a 250+ line component under any circumstance.

rules.name: Mandatory Component Decomposition Pattern
rules.description: Large components MUST be broken into a folder structure with a parent orchestrator and focused sub-components.

files: index.tsx — Orchestrator. Manages state, composes sub-components. No business logic.
ComponentHeader.tsx — Renders the header section only.
ComponentContent.tsx — Renders the main content section only.
ComponentFooter.tsx — Renders the footer section only.
useComponentLogic.ts — Custom hook containing all state and business logic.

examples.violation: Single 350-line MediaPlan.tsx with data fetching, state updates, table rendering, and export logic mixed together.
examples.compliant: MediaPlan/index.tsx (60 lines, composes sub-components) + MediaPlanHeader.tsx + MediaPlanTable.tsx + MediaPlanFooter.tsx + useMediaPlanData.ts

guidelines: Create a folder for the component: src/components/MediaPlan/
Parent index.tsx only orchestrates — it does NOT contain business logic or heavy JSX.
Sub-components receive data via props and emit events via callback props.
All useState/useEffect/useCallback logic lives in a matching custom hook file.

rules.name: Logic Abstraction with Custom Hooks
rules.description: Complex logic — any combination of useState, useEffect, useCallback, API calls — must be extracted into a named custom hook.
guidelines: If a component body has more than 2 useState calls, extract the state group into a custom hook.
If a component has fetch/async logic, it belongs in a hook, not the component.
The component body should be predominantly JSX — if it is more logic than markup, refactor.
Custom hooks live in src/hooks/ and are named with the 'use' prefix: useTheme, useExportHistory, usePosts.

examples.violation: A UserCard.tsx that contains useState for 5 fields, a useEffect for fetching, derived calculations, and 100 lines of JSX.
examples.compliant: useUserCard.ts handles all state and fetching. UserCard.tsx receives data from the hook and renders JSX only.

rules._ai_agent_note: A good test: if you removed all the logic from a component and only kept JSX, would it still be over 100 lines? If yes, the JSX itself needs to be decomposed into sub-components.

rules.name: Clear Prop-Driven Communication
rules.description: Components communicate via props (data down) and callbacks (events up). Avoid overusing Context for data that only one component needs.
guidelines: Favor composition over complex conditional rendering inside a single component.
Pass data and state downward as props.
Pass user events (onSubmit, onClick, onChange) upward via callback props.
Context is for truly global state (auth, theme, i18n) — not for feature-specific state.

examples.violation: A deeply nested component pulls 10 values directly from a global Context to avoid prop drilling, creating tight coupling.
examples.compliant: Parent component reads from Context and passes only the needed slice as props to the child component.

implementationProtocol.title: AI Agent Component Generation Protocol
implementationProtocol.description: Follow these steps in order every time you generate or modify a React component.

steps.description: Before writing a single line, define: What is the ONE thing this component does? What data does it need from props? What events does it emit?
Map out visible sections of the UI (header, body, list item, footer). Each section that would exceed 80 lines independently becomes its own sub-component.
Write the custom hook first. Define all state, effects, and derived values there. The hook should be testable in isolation.
Create ComponentName/ folder. Create index.tsx, sub-component files, and the custom hook file before writing any implementation.
Implement leaf sub-components first (they only receive props and emit callbacks). Keep them dumb and reusable.
Write index.tsx last. It calls the hook, receives data, and composes sub-components. Its JSX should be clean and declarative.
Count lines of each file. Verify: index.tsx < 100 lines, sub-components < 150 lines. Confirm exactly ONE responsibility per file. If any file exceeds limits, refactor before outputting.

list.fix: Decompose into a folder with orchestrator + at least 3 sub-components + 1 custom hook

list.violation: Putting API calls directly in the component body
list.fix: Extract all data-fetching into a dedicated custom hook using React Query

list.violation: Using 5+ useState hooks in a single component
list.fix: Group related state into a custom hook, or use useReducer with a well-typed action set

list.violation: Mixing rendering logic with complex conditional business rules
list.fix: Move business logic to the hook or a pure utility function; keep component JSX clean

list.violation: Components with names like 'Main', 'Page', 'Manager' containing everything
list.fix: Rename to reflect single purpose and decompose accordingly
